I have pocket tens with an ace kicker... wait, I have HT and HA - that's Ten of Hearts and Ace of Hearts. So I have Ace-Ten suited (both hearts). This is a strong hand - Ace-Ten suited is a premium hand preflop. The situation: Grok 4.1 fast has raised to 400 with only 240 in their stack, meaning they're essentially all-in (they put in 400 but only have 240 left, so they're all-in for 640 total? Wait - stack=240, bet=400. That means they went all-in for more than their stack? Let me reconsider - they have 240 remaining after betting 400, so they had 640 total and bet 400. The pot is 885. I need to call 280 more to win the pot. Pot odds: I'm calling 280 to win 885+280=1165, so I need about 24% equity. Ace-Ten suited has excellent equity against most ranges, especially against a short-stacked player who might be pushing with a wide range. Other players (Qwen3.5, GLM 5, DeepSeek 3.2) have also put in 120 each - they may call or raise behind me. With ATs, I should either call or raise. Raising would isolate against Grok who appears short-stacked and committed. A raise to isolate could be good here. However, with 3 other players still in (those who put in 120), I should raise to narrow the field. I'll raise to make it expensive for the limpers while having a strong hand.

However, Js Jh is vulnerable against overcards and I'm out of position. Folding seems too tight given the pot odds (pot is 1585, need 700, so about 2.26:1), but raising all-in could be an option to isolate or take it down now. Since Sonnet 4.6 has only 170 left after their raise, they are essentially all-in, so I'd be heads-up against Grok 4.1 if I raise. With 880 total, a shove might get Grok to fold marginal hands. Given the pot size and my hand strength, going all-in seems reasonable for fold equity and to avoid playing a multiway pot out of position with a marginal hand.

I have pocket Aces (HA, HT - wait, that's Ace of Hearts and Ten of Hearts, not pocket aces). Let me re-read: HT = Ten of Hearts, HA = Ace of Hearts. So I have A-T suited (hearts). I need to call 170 chips (my remaining stack) to potentially win a pot of 2705+170 = 2875. Pot odds: I'm calling 170 to win ~2875, which is about 17:1 odds. That's excellent pot odds. A-T suited is a strong hand. Even against a strong range, I'm rarely dominated badly. Against two all-in players, I could be behind (e.g., if someone has AA, KK, QQ, JJ, or AK, AQ, AJ), but with 17:1 pot odds, I need to win only about 6% of the time to break even. A-T suited will win much more than 6% even against strong hands. This is a clear call with these pot odds.

I have top two pair (Aces and Threes) on a board of J-2-A-9-3. This is an extremely strong hand, likely the best possible hand given the dry board texture. There are no obvious straights or flushes possible. My opponent has bet 50 into a 185 pot, which is a small blocking bet or value bet from a weaker hand. Folding is out of the question. Raising might fold out worse hands like a Jack or a bluff, so calling is the best play to keep them in and extract value, or potentially induce a bluff on a later street if there were one (though this is the river). Wait, this is the river, so no future streets. Calling is safe and wins against most of his range. Raising small could be good for value, but given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, a call ensures I don't scare him off if he has a marginal hand like AJ or KJ. However, with top two pair on the river, I should generally raise for value against any pair or bluff. But looking at the board, it's very dry. If he has a Jack, he calls a raise. If he has a bluff, he folds. If he has an Ace with a better kicker (unlikely given action), I lose. Actually, raising is better for value here. Let's re-evaluate. The pot is 185, he bets 50. Total pot 235. If I raise, I get more value from Jx, 9x, 2x. If I just call, I win 50. If I raise to say 150, he might call with Jx. Let's go with a raise for value. Wait, standard play with top two pair on river vs a small bet is often to raise. Let's raise to 150. Actually, let's look closer. Board: Jd 2h Ah 9s 3c. My hand: 3s Ac. I have Aces up. The only hands beating me are AA (impossible, I have one), 33 (unlikely he called pre and flop/turn with deuces?), JJ, 99, 22, or A9, A2, AJ. AJ is possible. 99, JJ are possible. But he bet small. Small bets often mean weak pairs or bluffs. If I raise, I fold out bluffs and weak pairs. If I call, I beat bluffs and weak pairs. Raising risks folding out the very hands I want to beat. But if he has AJ, he calls. If he has JJ, he calls or raises. Given the small bet size, he might be trapping with a monster or just blocking with a weak hand. Top two pair is strong enough to raise for value here. I'll raise to 140. Re-thinking: In many low-stakes or AI games, small bets on the river with top pair good kicker or two pair are often value bets from medium strength hands. Raising isolates me against better hands (sets, AJ) and folds out worse. Calling is the most balanced and profitable line here to catch bluffs and thin value from Jx. I will call.

The board is H2 C2 H9 C8 S8. My hand is DJ SK, giving me King-high with no pair, straight, or flush possibilities. All opponents except Grok 4.1 fast have folded. Since the current bet is 0 and I am heads-up against the last remaining player who has also checked (implied by bet=0 and it being my turn with no active bet), I should check to see if my King-high is good enough to win at showdown, as betting would likely only get called by better hands or fold out worse ones that might have bluffed. However, since the bet is 0 and it's my turn, checking is the standard play to realize equity or induce a bluff if I were to bet, but with King-high on this board, checking is safer to avoid building a pot I likely can't win if raised. Wait, actually, if everyone checked to the river and it's my turn with bet 0, I can either check or bet. Given my weak hand, checking is preferred to avoid putting more money in with likely the worst hand, hoping to win at showdown if Grok also has nothing.
